text: Hello and welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. I'm Sonika and today I have
Vijay with me from Vigod. Hello Vijay. Hi Sonika. Thank you for sharing your time here. Thanks for
having us here. Can you tell us a bit about Vigod? Vigod stands for water, electricity and gas of
things. It's an IoT platform built to measure and monitor utilities. Oh that's great. Looks to be
something with sustainability right? Right. Okay so can you tell us about what are we talking today?
So today I would like to walk through the Vigod Aqua which is the water management platform of Vigod.
Oh so let's straight dive into the architecture. Sure so before we start I would just let you know
that the architecture is split into three phases. So we have the ingestion layer which takes care of
the data coming in. We've got the pre-processing layer which manages the data and the post-processing
layer that saves the data. So today what we're talking about is close to 100k sensors. These are
water meters out in the field transmitting data to the AWS cloud. So the first point of contact is the
AWS API gateway which then forwards the information to a bunch of transformers. These are nothing but
lambdas that take the ingestion data and then process them. Why are they called transformers? We've got
three generations of sensors running out of the field and each talking its own protocol. So we need to
streamline them into a single protocol which is what the transformer does. So what the transformer
finally ends up doing is splitting every data into two segments. A flow data and an alarm data because
each data from the sensor essentially consists of these two components. How much of water is actually
flowing through the sensor and does the sensor have any alarm? Oh so 100k sensors right? I'm assuming it would be a lot of
events. How many events are we talking about? So we're talking about 27 million data points per day
which is what we ingest and process. Okay so 27 million events right? So once these many events of
data come I'm sure scalability is a big requirement here. Absolutely. So we've already taken care of the
scale with respect to the ingestion part because these lambdas scale based on the amount of data coming in.
We're really not worried about how many sensors the business adds. The second part of the scale is
managing that once the data has reached the cloud how do we manage it so that we don't slow down the
ingestion which is where the pre-processor comes in. So what the pre-processor does is it reads data
from these queues. These are the SQS VFO queues and makes an important decision as to what amount of data
needs to be really processed. The reason why I say this is a utility like water is not something we use 24 by 7.
So not all data points are warranted to be executed. So the pre-processor takes this decision in real time
looking into the data and decides what to process and what not to process. Now how does that do?
It works in conjunction with the elastic cache which is a high speed in-memory
Redis database that we use. And once it determines that hey this is the amount of water that or rather the
amount of data points that we need to process those are the only data points that get forwarded to the
post-process of processing. So by making intelligent decisions as to how much we need to process and
by allowing you know any amount of data to come in we essentially have built the architecture to
scale for any amount of sensors and any amount of data. This is like a real example of scalability
right and looks to be heart of your system. So what percentage of events are really worth you know to
share to the end users? In our experience I would say you know about 40 percent of the time series data that
we get is what goes into the final stage of processing. And how does the end user get the final view of how
efficiently are they using water? Yeah so once this 40 percent of data is allowed out they are further
processed and pushed into a post-processor. There are a bunch of jobs that run on an EC2 instance and they
end up getting saved in two data stores. So we've got two purpose-built data stores. One is the Aurora
serverless version of MySQL and the other is DynamoDB. The Aurora serverless version stores
a day data like how much of water was consumed in a particular sensor in a day. What are the alarms?
So these are basically daily statistics that get stored. DynamoDB on the other hand you know is
something that stores events. So why events and why daily data? We cater to both sources as well as
inlets in a society. Sources could be your tank lorries coming in, sources could be your borewells. So
there instead of the amount of water it's about when did the lorry dump water into the sump right? When did the
borewells start? Right? And in conjunction with the Aurora daily data both of this can be provided
via web APIs to our client web portals as well as the mobile apps. So essentially we have a web layer
that interacts between both of this and pushes the data to the end users consumption points which is
the app and the website. Okay so this is the architecture via which the end users really
understand how efficiently are they using water is it? Correct. So we have visualizations and reports that tell them
how they're performing and we also nudge them to perform better because the whole platform is
about rationalizing use of water and bringing in sustainability. That's a real good cause. So we
talked about 100k, you know 100k of sensors, 27 million events per day right? So how about cost?
How are you managing cost with this architecture? Yeah so with scale cost is also bound to increase
which is at least what we thought. Just to give you a perspective when we started this architecture we
were at about 15k sensors and today we're at about 100k. The revelation was that the cost actually went
down by 25 percent. So the cost of a sensor, the cloud cost per sensor is down 25 percent because our
lambdas are still not even hit 10 percent of concurrency and you know we have another 10x to grow
and because it is able to scale without a single change in the architecture or code we've been actually
able to realize so much of savings. That's really great and I hope that with your scalability you
further reduce this cost and the architecture looks great. Can you tell us about the other two product
lines right? So will this architecture also scale for the other two product lines of yours like electricity
and gas? Absolutely. So if you look at this to me this is a blueprint of an IoT system. Today it's water,
tomorrow it could be carrying data for electricity. The other product is basically about gas so that's
another data point. So I'm convinced that the entire architecture can be leveraged for both electricity
and gas and we hope to roll those product lines soon in the future. I hope too and Vijay this is a great
architecture with respect to what you're doing right? Sustainability for key resources like water, gas and
electricity. I hope the other two products come soon. Thank you so much for sharing your time and
explaining this architecture with us. Thank you for having us here. Thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
page 10GHT on camera.
